description: ClawHQ feature backlog — derived from docs/PRODUCT.md build phases

items.title: Project scaffolding and CLI entrypoint
items.description: Initialize the TypeScript/Node.js package, set up project structure per ARCHITECTURE.md, and create the root CLI entrypoint. This is the foundation everything else builds on.
steps: Run `npm init` and configure package.json with TypeScript, ESM output, and bin entry
Create project directory structure per ARCHITECTURE.md: src/cli/, src/config/, src/docker/, src/gateway/, src/security/, src/workspace/, src/templates/, src/backup/, src/doctor/, src/server/, src/ui/
Select CLI framework (commander, oclif, or citty — open decision) and create src/cli/index.ts as root entrypoint
Create version command that reads version from package.json
Configure tsconfig.json: strict mode, ESM output to dist/
Add package scripts: build (tsc), test (vitest), lint (eslint with TypeScript rules)
Ensure `npm run build` succeeds and `node dist/cli/index.js version` prints version info
Add .eslintrc with TypeScript-aware rules and import ordering
acceptanceCriteria: `npm run build` produces compiled output with zero TypeScript errors
`node dist/cli/index.js` prints help text listing available subcommands
`node dist/cli/index.js version` prints the version string
`npm test` passes (even if no tests yet, must not error)
Project structure matches the src/ layout defined in ARCHITECTURE.md
items.groomingNotes: Foundation item — everything depends on this. Keep minimal. IMPORTANT: Previous steps referenced Go/cobra — corrected to TypeScript/Node.js per ARCHITECTURE.md decision. CLI framework (commander/oclif/citty) is still an open decision per ARCHITECTURE.md § Open Decisions — Tech Lead must resolve before sprint starts.

items.title: Config loading and validation engine
items.description: Build the config loading system that reads/validates ClawHQ config (YAML) and OpenClaw config (JSON) using OpenClaw's TypeBox schema as the source of truth. This is the backbone of the doctor, init, and deploy commands.
steps: Create src/config/ package (schema.ts, loader.ts, validator.ts, generator.ts per ARCHITECTURE.md)
In schema.ts: import/re-export OpenClaw's TypeBox schema types — do NOT reimplement; this is the single source of truth for openclaw.json validation
In loader.ts: implement config loading from ~/.clawhq/config.yaml and ./clawhq.yaml with merge precedence (project > user > defaults)
In validator.ts: implement config validation using TypeBox schema for openclaw.json; implement the 14 landmine rules as a typed rule set (see PRODUCT.md § Config validation engine)
Validate() returns structured results: { rule, status: 'pass'|'warn'|'fail', message, fix } — not thrown errors
In generator.ts: stub out the DeploymentBundle generator (full implementation in FEAT-017/018)
Write unit tests for loading, merging, and each landmine rule (pass + fail cases)
Export a loadAndValidate() convenience function from src/config/index.ts
acceptanceCriteria: Config loads from YAML with correct precedence (project > user > defaults)
openclaw.json validation uses OpenClaw's TypeBox schema directly — no reimplementation
All 14 landmine validation rules are implemented and return clear fix instructions on failure
Invalid config produces structured results with fix instructions, not thrown exceptions
Unit tests cover each landmine rule with both passing and failing cases
`npm test src/config` passes with >80% coverage
items.groomingNotes: Critical path — doctor, init, and deploy all depend on config validation. IMPORTANT: Previous steps referenced Go structs — corrected to TypeScript/TypeBox per ARCHITECTURE.md. Key constraint: openclaw.json must be validated against OpenClaw's actual TypeBox schema (imported, not reimplemented) so ClawHQ-generated config passes Gateway validation automatically.

items.title: Docker client wrapper
items.description: Create a TypeScript wrapper around Docker CLI operations needed by ClawHQ: image builds, container lifecycle, health checks, network inspection. Subprocess-based (child_process/execa, not Docker SDK) per architecture decision.
steps: Create src/docker/ package (client.ts, build.ts, compose.ts, hardening.ts per ARCHITECTURE.md)
In client.ts: implement DockerClient class with methods: build, up, down, restart, ps, logs, inspect, networkLs — each wraps docker/docker-compose CLI via child_process or execa
Capture and parse stdout/stderr into typed result objects
Implement health polling: poll container health status with configurable timeout and interval using async/await
Implement image inspection: check if image exists, get layer info for two-stage build verification
Add typed error classes for common failures (DaemonNotRunning, ImageNotFound, PortConflict)
Write unit tests using subprocess mocking (jest.mock or vitest mock for child_process/execa)
acceptanceCriteria: DockerClient correctly wraps docker CLI commands via subprocess (not SDK)
Health polling returns structured status within configurable timeout
Errors are typed and actionable (not raw stderr strings)
Unit tests pass without requiring a running Docker daemon
All async methods accept AbortSignal for cancellation
items.groomingNotes: Build, deploy, and operate phases all need Docker operations. IMPORTANT: Previous steps referenced Go exec.Command — corrected to TypeScript child_process/execa. Subprocess-based approach (not Docker SDK) is per architecture decision — keeps the binary dependency surface minimal.

items.title: Doctor command — core diagnostic engine
items.description: Implement `clawhq doctor` — the hero feature. Runs all diagnostic checks and reports pass/warn/fail with fix instructions. This is the first command that delivers real value to Tinkerers.
steps: Create cmd/doctor.go with the doctor subcommand
Create internal/doctor/ package with a Check interface: Name, Run() -> CheckResult{Status, Message, Fix}
Implement check: Docker daemon running
Implement check: OpenClaw images exist
Implement check: Config validation (delegates to config.Validate())
Implement check: File permissions (.env is 600, identity files are read-only)
Implement check: Secrets not embedded in config files (scan openclaw.json for API key patterns)
Implement check: Container running and healthy (delegates to docker.HealthPoll())
Implement check: Port availability (18789 not already bound)
Implement the Runner that executes all checks and formats output as a table: check name | status | message
Add --json flag for machine-readable output
Add --fix flag that auto-fixes safe issues (permissions, firewall)
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq doctor` runs all checks and prints a formatted status table
Each check reports pass/warn/fail with specific fix instructions
`clawhq doctor --json` outputs structured JSON
`clawhq doctor --fix` auto-fixes permission issues
Exit code is non-zero if any check fails
Checks are modular (easy to add new ones via the Check interface)
items.groomingNotes: Hero feature. First real value for Tinkerer persona. Ship-blocking for Phase 1. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`cmd/doctor.go`, `internal/doctor/`) — these must be corrected to TypeScript src/ paths per ARCHITECTURE.md (e.g., `src/cli/doctor.ts`, `src/doctor/checks/`, `src/doctor/runner.ts`, `src/doctor/fix.ts`). Steps also reference a Go-style `Check` interface — should be a TypeScript interface. Correcting steps is Tech Lead scope.

items.title: Secrets management and credential health probes
items.description: Implement secrets management: .env file handling, credential health probes for connected integrations, and secret scanning across config files.
steps: Create internal/secrets/ package
Implement .env file reader/writer that preserves comments and ordering
Implement permission enforcement: ensure .env is chmod 600
Implement secret pattern scanner: regex patterns for API keys (sk-ant-*, ghp_*, AKIA*, Bearer tokens, JWTs)
Implement ScanFiles() that checks all config files for embedded secrets
Create internal/creds/ package for credential health probes
Implement health probe interface: Provider, Check() -> CredStatus{Valid, Expired, Failing, Error}
Implement probe for Anthropic API key (HEAD request to API)
Implement probe for OpenAI API key
Implement probe for Telegram bot token (getMe API call)
Create cmd/creds.go subcommand that runs all probes and displays status table
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq creds` checks all configured credentials and shows valid/expired/failing status
Secret scanner detects API keys embedded in non-.env files
.env permissions are enforced (warn if not 600)
Health probes timeout after 10s and don't crash on network errors
Unit tests cover pattern matching and .env read/write
items.groomingNotes: Security foundation. Doctor depends on this for its secrets checks. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`internal/secrets/`, `internal/creds/`, `cmd/creds.go`) — correct to TypeScript src/ paths per ARCHITECTURE.md (`src/security/secrets.ts`, `src/security/credentials.ts`, `src/cli/creds.ts`).

items.title: Container hardening and security posture
items.description: Implement automatic container hardening based on template security posture. Generate docker-compose.yml overrides with cap_drop ALL, read-only rootfs, non-root user, resource limits.
steps: Create internal/secure/ package
Define SecurityPosture enum: minimal, standard, hardened, paranoid
Implement docker-compose override generator per posture level
For 'hardened' (default): cap_drop ALL, read-only rootfs, no-new-privileges, non-root UID 1000, tmpfs with noexec/nosuid, ICC disabled, resource limits
Implement compose file merger: take base docker-compose.yml and apply security overrides
Add doctor check: verify running container matches expected security posture
Write unit tests for override generation and compose merging
acceptanceCriteria: Generated docker-compose override includes all hardening controls for the configured posture
Compose merger produces valid YAML that docker-compose accepts
Doctor can verify that running containers match the expected posture
Each posture level is documented with what controls it enables
Unit tests cover all four posture levels
items.groomingNotes: Security by default. This is what differentiates ClawHQ from hosting providers. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`internal/secure/`) — correct to TypeScript src/ paths per ARCHITECTURE.md (`src/docker/hardening.ts`, `src/docker/compose.ts`).

items.title: Egress firewall with domain allowlisting
items.description: Implement iptables-based egress firewall that restricts outbound traffic to allowlisted domains only. Stronger than 'allow all HTTPS' — enforces local-first at the network level.
steps: Create internal/firewall/ package
Implement domain-to-IP resolver for allowlist entries
Implement iptables chain manager: create/flush/delete CLAWHQ_FWD chain
Implement rule generator: ESTABLISHED/RELATED -> DNS(53) -> HTTPS(443) to allowlisted IPs only -> LOG+DROP
Derive allowlist from config: base domains (Docker Hub for pulls) + user cloud opt-in (api.anthropic.com, api.openai.com, etc.)
Implement Apply() that creates the chain and inserts rules via sudo iptables
Implement Remove() for clean teardown
Implement Verify() that checks current iptables rules match expected state
Add doctor check: firewall applied and matches expected rules
Handle macOS (pf) vs Linux (iptables) — or document Linux-only with clear error on macOS
acceptanceCriteria: Firewall creates a dedicated iptables chain with correct rule order
Only allowlisted domains can be reached over HTTPS
DNS (port 53) is allowed for resolution
Dropped packets are logged for debugging
Verify() returns true only when rules match expected state
Apply() is idempotent (safe to call multiple times)
items.groomingNotes: Core privacy commitment. Network-level enforcement of local-first principle. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`internal/firewall/`) — correct to TypeScript src/ paths per ARCHITECTURE.md (`src/security/firewall.ts`).

items.title: Two-stage Docker build command
items.description: Implement `clawhq build` with two-stage Docker build: Stage 1 (base OpenClaw + apt packages) only rebuilds when upstream changes, Stage 2 (custom tools + skills) completes in seconds.
steps: Create cmd/build.go with the build subcommand
Create internal/build/ package
Implement Stage 1 detection: check if base image exists and if upstream Dockerfile/apt packages changed
Implement Stage 1 build: docker build with OpenClaw Dockerfile and base dependencies
Implement Stage 2 build: docker build custom layer on top of Stage 1 with tools, skills, identity files
Generate build manifest: image hashes, layer sizes, build timestamps, binaries included
Add --stage2-only flag to skip Stage 1
Add --verify flag to compare current images against manifest
Display build progress and timing per stage
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq build` produces working container images from OpenClaw source
Stage 2 rebuilds complete in <30s when Stage 1 is cached
Build manifest is generated with image hashes and layer info
`clawhq build --verify` detects drift from manifest
`clawhq build --stage2-only` skips base image rebuild
items.groomingNotes: Auditability requirement — always build from source, never pre-built images. GAP resolved: FEAT-037 added to cover OpenClaw source acquisition. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`cmd/build.go`, `internal/build/`) — correct to TypeScript src/ paths per ARCHITECTURE.md (`src/cli/build.ts`, `src/docker/build.ts`).

items.title: Deploy sequence — clawhq up/down/restart
items.description: Implement the full deploy sequence: pre-flight checks, compose up, firewall apply, health poll, cron verify, channel connection verify, smoke test.
steps: Create cmd/up.go, cmd/down.go, cmd/restart.go
Create internal/deploy/ package
Implement pre-flight checks: Docker daemon, images exist, config valid, secrets present, ports available, Ollama reachable
Implement deploy sequence: compose up -> firewall apply -> health poll (60s timeout) -> report
Implement graceful shutdown: compose down preserving workspace state
Implement restart: down -> up with firewall reapply and health re-verify
Each pre-flight failure includes the exact fix command
Display deployment progress with step-by-step status
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq up` runs full pre-flight checks before starting containers
Pre-flight failures show exact fix instructions and abort deployment
Health poll waits up to 60s for container to be healthy
`clawhq down` stops containers gracefully
`clawhq restart` reapplies firewall and re-verifies health
Exit code is non-zero on any deployment failure
items.groomingNotes: Core deploy workflow. The one-command deploy experience. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`cmd/up.go`, `cmd/down.go`, `cmd/restart.go`, `internal/deploy/`) — correct to TypeScript src/ paths per ARCHITECTURE.md (`src/cli/up.ts`, `src/cli/down.ts`, `src/cli/restart.ts`, new `src/deploy/` package).

items.title: Status dashboard — clawhq status
items.description: Implement `clawhq status` — single-pane view of agent health, integrations, cost, cron, workspace metrics, and data egress summary.
steps: Create cmd/status.go with the status subcommand
Create internal/status/ package
Implement agent state section: container status, uptime, model loaded
Implement integration health section: credential status for each configured integration
Implement workspace metrics section: memory file sizes by tier, identity file token counts
Implement data egress section: bytes sent to cloud today/week/month, zero-egress badge
Format output as a clean terminal dashboard with sections and colors
Add --json flag for machine-readable output
Add --watch flag for live-updating display (refresh every 5s)
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq status` shows agent state, integration health, workspace metrics, and egress summary
Zero-egress badge displays when no cloud calls were made
`clawhq status --json` outputs structured JSON
`clawhq status --watch` updates every 5 seconds
Works gracefully when agent is not running (shows 'stopped' state)
items.groomingNotes: Tinkerer's window into their agent. Pairs with doctor for observability. TECH LEAD ACTION REQUIRED: Steps reference Go package paths (`cmd/status.go`, `internal/status/`) — correct to TypeScript src/ paths (`src/cli/status.ts`, new `src/status/` or inline in `src/server/`).

items.title: Local-first model routing engine
items.description: Implement the model routing engine that routes tasks to local Ollama models by default, with per-category cloud escalation policy. Core privacy commitment.
steps: Create internal/routing/ package
Define TaskCategory enum: research, email_triage, calendar, creative_writing, code_generation, general
Define RoutingPolicy struct: per-category rules with local_model_preference, cloud_allowed bool, cloud_provider, quality_threshold
Implement Ollama client: discover available models via `ollama list`, check model availability
Implement task complexity scoring: token count, required capabilities, template quality threshold
Implement Router.Route(task) -> ModelChoice: evaluates policy, picks best available model
Local models always tried first; cloud only if allowed for category AND local quality insufficient
Implement cost tracking: per-task attribution with model and provider
Write unit tests for routing decisions across policy configurations
acceptanceCriteria: Router picks local Ollama model by default for all task categories
Cloud escalation only occurs when explicitly allowed in category policy
With no cloud API keys configured, agent operates fully on local models
Task complexity scoring produces consistent results
Cost tracking attributes cost per task, per model
Unit tests cover: all-local, partial-cloud, full-cloud, no-ollama-fallback scenarios
items.groomingNotes: Architectural commitment to local-first. Affects every other feature that touches models. GAP: no item covers Ollama installation guidance or model pull setup. Doctor (FEAT-004) should detect Ollama absence and provide setup instructions. ARCHITECTURE CONTRADICTION — TECH LEAD MUST RESOLVE: ARCHITECTURE.md says 'OpenClaw handles model routing. ClawHQ generates config that tells OpenClaw how to route models. It does not make LLM calls itself.' But the steps describe a runtime Router.Route(task) -> ModelChoice engine with task complexity scoring and model selection — this is the routing engine, not config generation. If ClawHQ generates ModelRoutingPolicy config for OpenClaw to execute, the steps need to be reframed around config generation (define policy structs, write policy to openclaw.json via FEAT-036). If ClawHQ truly does its own routing (e.g. for init --smart or egress decisions), the architecture doc needs updating. ALSO: Steps reference Go package paths (`internal/routing/`) — correct to TypeScript `src/` paths.

items.title: Data egress logging and visibility
items.description: Implement egress logging: every outbound cloud API call is recorded with provider, model, token count, data category, and cost. Foundation for audit and status commands.
steps: Create internal/egress/ package
Define EgressRecord struct: timestamp, provider, model, token_count_in, token_count_out, data_category, cost, session_id
Implement append-only egress log writer (JSONL format)
Implement egress log reader with filtering: by date range, provider, category
Implement aggregation: total by provider (today/week/month), breakdown by category
Implement zero-egress check: verify no cloud calls in a given period
Create cmd/audit.go with `clawhq audit --egress` subcommand
Add --export flag to generate a signed egress report
acceptanceCriteria: Every cloud API call produces an egress record in the log
`clawhq audit --egress` shows per-call detail with provider, model, tokens, cost
Aggregation correctly sums by provider and category
Zero-egress attestation works for arbitrary time periods
Log format is append-only JSONL for integrity
items.groomingNotes: Privacy transparency. Feeds into status dashboard and audit command.

items.title: Encrypted backup and restore
items.description: Implement `clawhq backup` and `clawhq backup restore` — encrypted snapshots of agent state with GPG encryption, integrity verification, and restore with doctor validation.
steps: Create cmd/backup.go with backup and backup restore subcommands
Create internal/backup/ package
Implement snapshot collection: workspace, config, credentials (.env), cron, identity files
Implement GPG encryption of snapshot archive
Generate backup manifest: file list, sizes, hashes, timestamp, backup ID
Implement backup listing: show available backups with IDs and timestamps
Implement restore: decrypt, validate integrity, extract, run doctor, verify agent starts
Add --secrets-only flag to backup only sensitive files
Store backups in configurable location (default: ~/.clawhq/backups/)
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq backup` creates an encrypted snapshot with all agent state
`clawhq backup restore <id>` restores from snapshot and verifies via doctor
Backup manifest includes integrity hashes for all files
Restore fails cleanly if integrity check fails
`clawhq backup --secrets-only` backs up only .env and credential files
Backups are listed with IDs and timestamps
items.groomingNotes: Disaster recovery. Required before safe-update can be implemented.

items.title: Safe upstream update — clawhq update
items.description: Implement `clawhq update` — upstream OpenClaw updates with pre-update snapshot, rebuild, health check, firewall reapply, and instant rollback on failure.
steps: Create cmd/update.go with the update subcommand
Create internal/update/ package
Implement upstream version check: fetch latest OpenClaw release tag
Implement changelog display with breaking changes highlighted
Implement pre-update snapshot via backup system
Implement update sequence: snapshot -> pull -> rebuild -> stop -> start -> healthcheck -> firewall reapply -> doctor
Implement rollback: on any failure, restore previous image, restart, verify
Add --check flag to show what would change without updating
Add --force flag to skip confirmation
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq update` shows changelog and confirms before updating
Pre-update snapshot is created automatically
On any failure during update, rollback restores the previous state
Rollback uses previous image (no rebuild needed) for instant recovery
`clawhq update --check` shows diff without modifying anything
Doctor runs after update to verify health
items.groomingNotes: Safety net for staying current with OpenClaw. Depends on backup and build.

items.title: Intelligent cost routing and budget management
items.description: Implement cost tracking with per-task attribution, budget alerts, and automatic routing adjustment when budget thresholds are hit.
steps: Create internal/cost/ package
Implement per-model cost tables (local = $0, Haiku/Sonnet/Opus rates per token)
Implement budget configuration: monthly cap, alert thresholds (75%, 100%)
Implement cost attribution per task category and per model
Implement budget monitoring: track spend against cap with projections
At 75% budget: shift eligible tasks from cloud to local models before alerting
At 100% budget: cloud-escalation stops entirely, agent operates local-only
Integrate into status dashboard: cost breakdown section
Write unit tests for routing adjustments at budget thresholds
acceptanceCriteria: Cost is tracked per-task with model, provider, and category attribution
Budget threshold at 75% triggers automatic routing adjustment
Budget threshold at 100% blocks all cloud escalation (agent continues local-only)
Status dashboard shows cost breakdown by model and category
Agent never pauses completely due to budget — always falls back to local
items.groomingNotes: Upgraded must (was should): PRODUCT.md rates this P0. Budget enforcement is the concrete proof that local-first works — without it, users have no feedback loop on what's actually staying local vs. escalating to cloud. The 100% budget cap (agent continues local-only, never pauses) is a critical trust commitment. Pairs with FEAT-012 (egress logging) to make the full transparency story real.

items.title: Health self-repair (auto-recovery)
items.description: Implement automatic recovery from common failures: network drops, Gateway crashes, bridge interface changes. Reduces operational burden on Tinkerers.
steps: Create internal/repair/ package
Implement health monitor: periodic check of container, network, and firewall state
Implement auto-reconnect: detect network drop, retry connection
Implement Gateway crash recovery: detect unhealthy container, auto-restart
Implement firewall reapply: detect bridge interface change, reapply iptables rules
Log all auto-repair actions for audit trail
Implement `clawhq repair` command for manual trigger of all recovery checks
Add configurable enable/disable for each auto-repair behavior
acceptanceCriteria: Gateway crash triggers automatic container restart
Network drop triggers reconnection attempt
Bridge interface change triggers firewall reapply
All auto-repair actions are logged
Auto-repair can be disabled per-behavior in config
items.groomingNotes: Downgraded could (was should): PRODUCT.md rates this P1 and existing groomingNotes say 'not ship-blocking'. The Tinkerer persona can manually restart/reapply — `clawhq restart` covers the gap. Self-repair is valuable but Phase 1 must ship first. Revisit after Phase 1 ships and Tinkerer feedback shows whether this is a real pain point.

items.title: Init wizard — clawhq init with guided questionnaire
items.description: Implement `clawhq init --guided` — structured setup flow that walks through basics, template selection, integration setup with credential validation, and config generation.
steps: Create cmd/init.go with the init subcommand
Create internal/init/ package
Implement Step 1: basics — name, timezone, waking hours
Implement Step 2: template selection — present use-case templates with descriptions
Implement Step 3: integration setup — per-integration credential input with live validation
Implement Step 4: model routing — local-only or cloud opt-in per category
Implement Step 5: config generation — produce openclaw.json, .env, docker-compose.yml, identity files, cron/jobs.json
Run config validation (all 14 landmine rules) before writing files
Display summary of generated config and next steps
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq init --guided` walks through all setup steps interactively
Credentials are validated live before proceeding
Generated config passes all 14 landmine validation rules
All generated files are written atomically (no partial writes on failure)
Summary shows what was configured and the next command to run
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 start. Requires config, secrets, and security foundations.

items.title: Use-case templates (YAML operational profiles)
items.description: Implement the template system: YAML-based operational profiles organized by what users are replacing (Google Assistant, ChatGPT Plus, PA, etc.). Templates map use-case framing to operational dimensions.
steps: Create internal/template/ package

Define Template struct: name, version, use_case_mapping, personality, security_posture, monitoring, memory_policy, cron_config, autonomy_model, model_routing_strategy, integration_requirements, skill_bundle
Create 6 built-in templates as YAML files in configs/templates/: replace-google-assistant, replace-chatgpt-plus, replace-my-pa, family-hub, research-copilot, founders-ops
Implement template loader with validation
Implement template preview: display what integrations are needed, autonomy model, local model requirements, estimated daily cost, and 'day in the life' narrative
Implement template-to-config mapper: generate DeploymentBundle from Template + setup answers
Write unit tests for template loading and config generation
acceptanceCriteria: 6 built-in templates exist with complete operational profiles
Templates load and validate from YAML without error
Template preview shows all relevant info including cost estimates
Template-to-config mapper produces valid DeploymentBundle
Templates can tighten but never loosen Layer 1 security baselines
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 — use-case framing is the key differentiator for Privacy Migrants.

items.title: AI-powered config inference — clawhq init --smart
items.description: Implement natural language config inference: user describes what they want, local LLM maps it to template selection and override parameters.
steps: Create internal/inference/ package
Implement prompt construction: system prompt that understands templates and operational dimensions
Implement Ollama integration for local LLM inference
Implement response parser: extract template selection, integration requirements, autonomy levels, boundaries
Implement conversational refinement: show plain-language summary, allow adjustments
Implement fallback to guided questionnaire when local model isn't available
Generate config from inference results using template-to-config mapper
acceptanceCriteria: User describes needs in plain language and gets a valid config proposal
Inference runs on local Ollama model (no cloud dependency)
User can adjust any aspect conversationally before generating
Falls back to guided questionnaire if Ollama isn't available
Generated config passes all validation rules
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 — the 5-minute setup experience for Privacy Migrants. Natural language config inference is a major adoption enabler: instead of answering 15 questions, user describes what they want. DEPENDENCY FIX NEEDED: FEAT-011 is in dependsOn but is wontdo. init --smart uses Ollama directly (localhost:11434) for LLM inference — not a routing engine. Tech Lead: remove FEAT-011 from dependsOn array. Real prerequisites are FEAT-017 (init wizard) + FEAT-018 (templates), both done. This item is unblocked now.

items.title: Identity governance — token budget and drift detection
items.description: Implement identity file governance: track token budget per file, detect staleness, flag contradictions across identity files.
steps: Create internal/identity/ package
Implement token counter for identity files (approximate, using tiktoken-go or word-based estimation)
Implement budget tracking: per-file token count vs bootstrapMaxChars (default 20K)
Implement warnings at 70% and 90% budget thresholds
Implement staleness detection: flag files not updated in configurable period
Implement consistency checker: parse identity files for contradictions (using local LLM)
Add identity section to doctor checks
Create cmd/evolve.go with `clawhq evolve --identity` subcommand for identity review
acceptanceCriteria: Token budget is tracked per identity file with threshold warnings
Staleness detection flags files that haven't been updated
Consistency checker identifies contradictions across identity files
Doctor reports identity health in its output
`clawhq evolve --identity` triggers interactive identity review
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 Evolve — prevents identity drift over time.

items.title: Memory lifecycle management — structured knowledge graph
items.description: Implement tiered memory management: transform raw conversations into structured knowledge, manage hot/warm/cold tiers with LLM-powered summarization, background reflection.
steps: Create internal/memory/ package
Define StructuredMemory types: preferences (hierarchical), relationships (cross-linked), domain_expertise, context
Implement memory ingestion: parse raw conversation logs into structured knowledge entries
Implement hot tier: recent structured knowledge, <100KB, <7 days
Implement warm tier: LLM-summarized knowledge, PII-aware, <90 days
Implement cold tier: further compressed, PII-masked, >90 days (or delete per policy)
Implement tier transitions with local model summarization
Implement background reflection: analyze existing memories for new connections during idle time
Add memory section to status dashboard and doctor checks
acceptanceCriteria: Raw conversations are transformed into structured knowledge (not stored as flat logs)
Hot -> warm tier transition summarizes while preserving key information
Warm -> cold tier further compresses and masks PII
Background reflection detects and proposes new connections
All summarization runs on local models by default
Memory health is reported in doctor and status
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 Evolve — biggest differentiator for agent longevity. Raw OpenClaw agents fail because memory bloats to 360KB in 3 days. This is what keeps agents working past month 1. DEPENDENCY FIX NEEDED: FEAT-011 is in dependsOn but is wontdo. Memory lifecycle is filesystem management of OpenClaw workspace files — reading conversation logs, restructuring them, managing hot/warm/cold file tiers. LLM summarization for tier transitions calls Ollama directly (localhost:11434). Tech Lead: remove FEAT-011 from dependsOn. Real dependency: FEAT-002 (config) only. This item is unblocked.

items.title: Activity digest and approval queue
items.description: Implement daily activity digest and approval queue for high-stakes agent actions. The transparency layer that builds user trust.
steps: Create internal/digest/ package

Implement digest generator: local LLM summarizes activity into human-readable daily summary
Implement privacy mode: summarize by category without showing content
Create internal/approval/ package
Implement approval queue: actions in requires-approval categories are queued
Implement approval resolution: approve, reject (with reason as preference signal), expire after timeout
Create cmd/digest.go subcommand
Integrate approval queue with messaging channel (Telegram)
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq digest` shows human-readable activity summary

Privacy mode summarizes by category without exposing content
Approval queue works for high-stakes actions (send email, create event, etc.)
Rejected approvals store reason as preference signal
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 Transparency — the daily summary builds user trust with Privacy Migrants. Without it, the agent is a black box and they can't verify it's working as intended. DEPENDENCY FIX NEEDED: Both FEAT-011 and FEAT-012 are in dependsOn but are wontdo. Activity digest reads container logs and workspace state directly. Egress summary reads iptables LOG entries (see FEAT-059). LLM summarization calls Ollama directly. Tech Lead: replace dependsOn with [FEAT-036, FEAT-010] — Gateway WebSocket (live state) and status dashboard (data sources). Approval queue is messaging-channel-based and standalone.

items.title: Portable export — clawhq export

Implement --mask-pii flag: apply PII masking throughout
Implement --no-memory flag: export only identity + config
Package as tar.gz with integrity manifest

--mask-pii applies PII masking to all files
--no-memory exports only identity and config
Bundle integrity can be verified via manifest hashes
items.groomingNotes: Upgraded must: 'zero lock-in' is a first-class trust commitment, not a nice-to-have. Privacy Migrants choosing ClawHQ over hosted services need to know they can leave at any time. Without export, the product contradicts its own positioning.

items.title: Verified destruction — clawhq destroy

Require deployment name typed out for confirmation

Generate signed destruction manifest with timestamps and verification hashes
Add --keep-export flag to preserve export bundle

items.title: PII and secret scanning — clawhq scan

Implement secret patterns: ghp_*, sk-ant-*, AKIA*, Bearer tokens, JWTs, private keys
Implement dangerous filename detection: .env, *.pem, *.key
Implement false positive filtering: CHANGE_ME placeholders, env var references, comments

Format output as table with file, line, pattern type, and match preview (redacted)

items.title: Channel connection — clawhq connect

Implement WhatsApp setup flow: walk through Business API setup, validate credentials
Implement --test flag: send test message and verify response (bidirectional)

items.title: Preference learning from corrections
items.description: Implement preference learning: classify user corrections (preference, boundary, one-time), accumulate signals, propose identity file updates after threshold.

Implement correction classifier: determine if correction is preference, boundary, or one-time override
Implement signal accumulator: track signals per category
Implement threshold proposer: when 5+ signals in same category, propose preference update
Implement identity file updater: apply approved preference updates with rollback capability
Log all preference changes for audit trail
acceptanceCriteria: Corrections are classified into preference, boundary, or one-time override
After 5 signals in the same category, a preference update is proposed
User approves preference updates before they're applied
Applied preferences are logged with rollback capability
Boundary signals are never overridden by preference signals
items.groomingNotes: Phase 2 Evolve — the 'gets better over time' mechanism.

items.title: Autonomy tuning from approval patterns

Implement recommendation engine: propose auto-approve when >95% approval rate, propose require-approval when >50% rejection rate
Implement cooldown: don't re-recommend rejected changes for configurable period
Implement policy updater: apply approved autonomy changes to approval policy
Log all autonomy changes for audit trail
acceptanceCriteria: Approval patterns are analyzed per action category

items.title: Gateway WebSocket client

Implement request/response RPC pattern with typed message envelopes and timeout handling
In health.ts: implement health check polling against :18789/healthz — Gateway must be healthy before further operations
In config-rpc.ts: implement config.patch and config.apply wrappers that respect the rate limit (3 req/60s)
Implement graceful fallback: when Gateway is down, direct filesystem write is used instead of RPC
Handle connection errors, auth failures, and rate limit responses with typed error classes
Write unit tests with a mock WebSocket server
acceptanceCriteria: WebSocket client authenticates to Gateway and sends/receives typed RPC messages

config.patch respects 3 req/60s rate limit (queues or errors on excess)

Doctor (FEAT-004), Status (FEAT-010), and Deploy (FEAT-009) all depend on Gateway communication. Without this foundation, those items cannot be built correctly. Added to phase1 and marked readyForSprint — dependency chain: FEAT-001 → FEAT-036 → FEAT-004/009/010.

items.title: OpenClaw source acquisition and version pinning

Implement source fetcher: download/clone OpenClaw source at configured version to a local cache directory
Implement version pinning: store pinned OpenClaw version in ClawHQ config; reject builds that would use a different version without explicit override
Implement integrity verification: checksum or GPG signature check on downloaded source
Implement source cache management: detect when cached source matches pinned version (skip re-download)
Add doctor check: pinned OpenClaw version is available and verified
Surface current OpenClaw version in `clawhq status` output
acceptanceCriteria: ClawHQ can acquire OpenClaw source at a pinned version without manual intervention

items.groomingNotes: GAP identified in FEAT-008 grooming notes: 'no backlog item covers how ClawHQ acquires the OpenClaw source (git clone, release tarball, version pinning).' This is a prerequisite for FEAT-008 (two-stage Docker build). Marked readyForSprint: false because the acquisition strategy (git vs. tarball) is an open decision that must be resolved first. Also affects FEAT-014 (upstream updates) which needs version comparison logic.

items.title: Skill management — clawhq skill install/list/update/remove
items.description: Implement safe skill lifecycle management: install, list, update, and remove OpenClaw skills with sandboxed vetting and rollback capability. The safety layer (sandboxing, vetting, rollback) is what differentiates ClawHQ from raw OpenClaw skill installation.
steps: Create src/cli/skill.ts with skill subcommand group
Implement `clawhq skill list`: show installed skills with version, source, status (active/disabled), last-used timestamp

Implement `clawhq skill install <name|url>`: fetch skill, sandbox in isolated container, scan for suspicious patterns (outbound network calls, filesystem access outside workspace, credential access), present for user approval before activation
On approval: update TOOLS.md, trigger Stage 2 rebuild if skill requires container-level dependencies
Implement `clawhq skill remove <name>`: deactivate skill, remove from TOOLS.md, keep rollback snapshot for 30 days
Implement `clawhq skill update <name>`: sandbox and vet new version before replacing; old version kept as rollback
Write unit tests for skill vetting logic and lifecycle operations
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq skill install` sandboxes and vets skill before presenting user approval prompt
User must explicitly approve before skill is activated — no auto-install
TOOLS.md is updated correctly on install and remove
`clawhq skill remove` provides 30-day rollback window
`clawhq skill update` vets new version before replacing old — old version is rollback target
items.groomingNotes: MISSING ITEM: Phase 2 Evolve — `clawhq skill install/list/update/remove` is a P0 story in PRODUCT.md (§ Skill management) but had no backlog entry. This is a core Evolve feature: the agent ships with a baseline skill set and grows through user-approved skill installations. The safety layer (sandboxing, vetting, rollback) is the key differentiator from raw OpenClaw. No readyForSprint until FEAT-008 (two-stage build) ships — skill install depends on Stage 2 rebuild working correctly.

items.title: Integration management — clawhq integrate add/remove/swap/list
items.description: Implement service integration lifecycle: add, remove, swap, and list integrations (email, calendar, tasks, etc.) with provider abstraction so the agent talks to 'calendar' not 'Google Calendar.' Swapping provider changes the backend without changing agent behavior.
steps: Create src/cli/integrate.ts with integrate subcommand group
Implement `clawhq integrate add <type>`: walk through provider selection (e.g., Google Calendar vs. iCloud vs. CalDAV), credential setup with live validation, config generation
On add: write credential to .env, install integration tool, update TOOLS.md, check cron dependencies, update egress firewall allowlist for provider's API domains, run targeted doctor health check

Implement `clawhq integrate remove <name>`: clean credential from .env, uninstall tool, update TOOLS.md, update identity files to remove references, flag orphaned cron dependencies, tighten firewall allowlist
Implement `clawhq integrate swap <category> <new-provider>`: change backend provider (e.g., `integrate swap calendar icloud`) while preserving agent behavior — same category interface, new backend
Implement `clawhq integrate list`: show all integrations with provider, status, credential health, last-used
Write unit tests for integration lifecycle and firewall allowlist management
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq integrate add` validates credentials live before completing setup
Adding an integration atomically updates egress firewall allowlist for that provider's domains
`clawhq integrate swap` changes provider without changing agent's task behavior (same category interface)
`clawhq integrate remove` tightens firewall allowlist and cleans all config references
`clawhq integrate list` shows credential health status per integration
items.groomingNotes: MISSING ITEM: Phase 2 Evolve — `clawhq integrate` is a P0 story in PRODUCT.md (§ Integration management) but had no backlog entry. The provider abstraction ('calendar' not 'Google Calendar') is Layer 3 of the product model. KEY RISK: swap operation must update firewall allowlist atomically — old provider domains must be removed when new provider is added, not left allowlisted. Credential health check on `integrate add` is required (reuse FEAT-005 probe infrastructure).

items.title: API provider management — clawhq provider add/list/remove/test
items.description: Implement LLM and service API provider lifecycle: add providers with per-category task opt-in, list with egress stats, remove with routing policy update and firewall tightening, test with live inference call.
steps: Create src/cli/provider.ts with provider subcommand group
Implement `clawhq provider add <name>`: API key setup with live validation, configure which task categories this provider is allowed for, update egress firewall allowlist for provider's API domains

Implement `clawhq provider list`: show all providers with credential status (valid/expired/failing), allowed task categories, egress stats (calls today/week/month, tokens sent, cost), routing priority

Implement `clawhq provider remove <name>`: clean credential from .env, update model routing config to exclude provider, tighten firewall allowlist, reroute tasks to next available provider or local fallback
Implement `clawhq provider test <name>`: run test inference call to verify provider end-to-end
Write unit tests for provider lifecycle and routing config updates
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq provider add` validates credential live and requires per-category opt-in before activating
Adding a provider updates egress firewall allowlist for that provider's API domains
`clawhq provider remove` removes from routing config and tightens firewall (not just credential delete)
`clawhq provider test` verifies live end-to-end connectivity with a real inference call

items.groomingNotes: MISSING ITEM: Phase 2 Evolve — `clawhq provider` is a P0 story in PRODUCT.md (§ API provider management) but had no backlog entry. IMPORTANT architectural note: adding a provider does NOT automatically route traffic to it — the user must configure per-category opt-in (done during `provider add` flow). Provider management is the user-facing control for the model routing config generated via FEAT-011/FEAT-015. Egress stat display links to FEAT-012 (egress logging).

items.title: CLI tool installation — clawhq tool install/list/remove

`clawhq tool install` triggers Stage 2 rebuild automatically after Dockerfile update
`clawhq tool list` shows base image tools and user-added tools separately
`clawhq tool remove` removes package from Dockerfile and triggers rebuild
items.groomingNotes: MISSING ITEM: Phase 2 Evolve — `clawhq tool install/list/remove` is a P1 story in PRODUCT.md (§ CLI tool installation) but had no backlog entry. This is a thin wrapper around the two-stage build: it modifies the Stage 2 Dockerfile and triggers a rebuild. Priority: should (not must) because the agent is useful without extra tools; tool installation is for power users expanding capability. Depends on FEAT-008 being solid — don't ship this until Stage 2 rebuild is reliable.

items.title: Evolve rollback — clawhq evolve rollback/history
items.description: Implement rollback capability for all Evolve changes (skill install, integration add, provider add, tool install, identity change). Every Evolve action creates a rollback snapshot. Expanding agent capabilities is never a one-way door.
steps: Create src/workspace/evolve-history.ts for change tracking

Implement rollback snapshot creation on every Evolve action — call before applying change
Implement `clawhq evolve rollback <change-id>`: reverse specific change by restoring files, reverting config, triggering image rebuild if needed
Implement `clawhq evolve history`: show all Evolve changes with IDs, timestamps, change type, and rollback status (available/expired)
Rollback snapshots expire after 30 days
Write unit tests for rollback mechanics across all change types
acceptanceCriteria: Every Evolve action (skill, integration, provider, tool, identity) automatically creates a rollback snapshot before applying
`clawhq evolve rollback <id>` fully reverses the named change
Rollback triggers Stage 2 rebuild when the reversed change affected container image

`clawhq evolve history` shows complete change history with rollback availability
items.groomingNotes: MISSING ITEM: Phase 2 Evolve — `clawhq evolve rollback` is a P1 story in PRODUCT.md (§ Evolve rollback) but had no backlog entry. This is the safety net that makes capability expansion non-scary. Without rollback, every skill install or integration add is a one-way door and users will hesitate. Depends on FEAT-038/039/040/041 all having consistent snapshot infrastructure — the rollback mechanism must be uniform across all Evolve change types.

items.title: Supply chain security — vetting pipeline for skills and tools

Separation of concerns: FEAT-038/041 handle lifecycle operations and include basic allowlist gating; this item adds the deeper vetting layer (AI scanning, VirusTotal, audit) on top. The `clawhq evolve audit` command also lives here — it's the transparency mechanism for all capability changes.

items.title: Secrets CLI — add and list commands
items.description: Implement `clawhq secrets add <name>` (masked input, optional health probe validation) and `clawhq secrets list` (names, provider category, health status, age, last rotated — never values). Builds on FEAT-005's env.ts and credential health probes.
steps: Create src/cli/secrets.ts with `secrets` subcommand and `add`/`list` sub-subcommands
Implement `secrets add <name>`: prompt for value with masked input (no echo), write to .env via existing parseEnv/serializeEnv in src/security/secrets/env.ts, enforce chmod 600

Implement `secrets list`: read .env, display table with columns: name, provider_category (inferred from key name patterns), health_status (via credential probes), age (from metadata), last_rotated — never display values
Create src/security/secrets/metadata.ts to track secret metadata (created_at, rotated_at, provider_category) in a sidecar JSON file (.env.meta) with 600 permissions
Add SecretEntry type definition in src/security/secrets/types.ts matching PRODUCT.md data model
Write unit tests for add flow (masked input mock, .env write, permission enforcement) and list display formatting
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq secrets add MY_KEY` prompts for masked input and writes to .env with 600 permissions
`clawhq secrets add MY_KEY --validate` runs health probe after writing and reports valid/invalid
`clawhq secrets list` shows all secrets with name, category, health, age — never shows values
Secret metadata (.env.meta) tracks created_at and provider_category
Unit tests cover .env write, metadata tracking, and list formatting
items.groomingNotes: First of four items implementing the expanded Secrets lifecycle management story from PRODUCT.md § Secure. Builds directly on FEAT-005's existing env.ts and credential health probes. The SecretEntry type and .env.meta sidecar pattern establish the foundation for rotate/revoke/audit in subsequent items.

items.title: Secrets CLI — rotate and revoke commands
items.description: Implement `clawhq secrets rotate <name>` (atomic swap, health validation, audit event) and `clawhq secrets revoke <name>` (remove from .env, verify removal, warn on dangling references).
steps: Add `rotate` and `revoke` sub-subcommands to src/cli/secrets.ts
Implement `secrets rotate <name>`: prompt for new value (masked), validate via health probe, atomically swap in .env (write to .env.tmp then rename), update rotated_at in .env.meta, emit audit event
Implement atomic .env swap in src/security/secrets/env.ts: write new content to .env.tmp with 600 permissions, then fs.rename() for atomic replacement

Implement `secrets revoke <name>`: remove entry from .env, verify removal by re-reading, scan all config files (openclaw.json, docker-compose.yml, workspace configs) for `${NAME}` references and warn if found
Implement dangling reference scanner in src/security/secrets/references.ts: glob config files, regex match `${SECRET_NAME}` and `$SECRET_NAME` patterns
Emit audit events for rotate and revoke (write to audit trail — consumed by FEAT-046)
Write unit tests for atomic swap, revocation, dangling reference detection
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq secrets rotate MY_KEY` prompts for new value, validates, and atomically swaps in .env
Rotation updates rotated_at metadata and emits audit event
`clawhq secrets revoke MY_KEY` removes from .env and confirms removal
Revoke warns if config files still reference `${MY_KEY}`
Atomic swap uses write-to-tmp-then-rename pattern (no partial writes on crash)
items.groomingNotes: Second secrets lifecycle item. Depends on FEAT-044 for SecretEntry types, metadata sidecar, and CLI scaffolding. Atomic swap pattern is critical — .env corruption during rotate would break the agent. Audit events are emitted here but the audit trail storage (FEAT-046) can be implemented in parallel if needed.

items.title: Secrets audit trail — append-only JSONL with HMAC chain
items.description: Implement `clawhq secrets audit` command and the underlying append-only JSONL audit trail with HMAC chain for tamper detection. Logs all secret events: added, rotated, revoked, accessed-by-deploy.
steps: Create src/security/secrets/audit.ts with SecretAuditEvent type matching PRODUCT.md data model (seq, event_type, secret_name, timestamp, prev_hmac, hmac)
Implement append-only JSONL writer: each line is a JSON SecretAuditEvent, file permissions 600
Implement HMAC chain: each event's hmac = HMAC-SHA256(key, JSON(event_without_hmac) + prev_hmac), first event uses empty prev_hmac
Implement chain verification: read all events, recompute HMAC chain, report any tampering
Add `secrets audit` sub-subcommand to src/cli/secrets.ts: display chronological event log with formatted table (seq, timestamp, event_type, secret_name)
Add `--verify` flag to `secrets audit` that validates HMAC chain integrity
Integrate audit event emission into FEAT-044 add/list and FEAT-045 rotate/revoke flows (export appendAuditEvent function)
Emit accessed-by-deploy events from the deploy pipeline (hook into src/docker/ deploy flow)
Write unit tests for HMAC chain computation, verification, and tamper detection
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq secrets audit` displays chronological log of all secret events
Audit trail is append-only JSONL file with 600 permissions
Each event has HMAC linking to previous event (tamper-evident chain)
`clawhq secrets audit --verify` validates chain integrity and reports tampering
Events are recorded for: add, rotate, revoke, and deploy-access operations
items.groomingNotes: Third secrets lifecycle item. Only hard dependency is FEAT-044 for types — can be built in parallel with FEAT-045. The HMAC chain uses a local key stored alongside the audit file (not high-security HSM, but detects casual tampering and accidental corruption). Deploy-access event emission requires a hook in the deploy pipeline.

items.title: Secrets export/import and encrypted storage backend
items.description: Implement `clawhq secrets export/import` for encrypted backup/restore of secrets, and an encrypted storage backend (AES-256-GCM) as upgrade path from plaintext .env. SecretStore interface abstracts both backends.
steps: Create src/security/secrets/store.ts with SecretStore interface: get(name), set(name, value), delete(name), list(), export(), import()
Implement PlaintextEnvStore as SecretStore wrapping existing env.ts parseEnv/serializeEnv — this is the default backend
Implement EncryptedStore as SecretStore: secrets encrypted with AES-256-GCM via Node.js crypto, master key derived from user passphrase via scrypt, stored in .env.enc
Add `secrets export` sub-subcommand: prompt for encryption passphrase, encrypt all secrets to portable archive (.clawhq-secrets.enc), include metadata and audit trail
Add `secrets import` sub-subcommand: prompt for passphrase, decrypt archive, verify integrity (HMAC), merge or overwrite into current .env with conflict prompts
Add `--encrypted` flag to `clawhq secrets` to enable encrypted backend (migrates existing .env to .env.enc, securely wipes plaintext .env)
Integrate with deploy pipeline: decrypt-to-tmpfs at deploy time (mount secrets as tmpfs volume, decrypt on container start, never write plaintext to disk)
Write unit tests for encryption round-trip, export/import cycle, backend switching
acceptanceCriteria: `clawhq secrets export` creates encrypted archive of all secrets with passphrase protection
`clawhq secrets import` restores secrets from encrypted archive with integrity verification
SecretStore interface abstracts storage — plaintext .env and encrypted backends are interchangeable
`--encrypted` flag migrates from plaintext to encrypted storage and securely wipes old .env
Deploy pipeline decrypts to tmpfs (secrets never written to persistent disk in encrypted mode)
items.groomingNotes: Fourth and final secrets lifecycle item. The SecretStore interface is the key architectural piece — it ensures FEAT-044/045/046 work identically regardless of backend. Export/import enables migration between machines. Encrypted backend is an upgrade path, not a replacement — plaintext .env must remain supported for simplicity. Depends on FEAT-046 because export bundles the audit trail.

items.title: Template-driven workspace channels
items.description:
The tasks tool defaults to 12 channels hardcoded from the Clawdius deployment (developer, trader, pa, health, security, family, kahu, simon-growth, clawdius-growth, content, finances, network). These are meaningless for other agents. Each template should define channels appropriate to its use case. The channel list flows into the tasks tool generator, TOOLS.md documentation, and work-recon skill.

Suggested channel sets per template:
- Replace Google Assistant: personal, work, shopping, health, family, home, errands
- Replace ChatGPT Plus: research, writing, coding, learning
- Replace my PA: inbox, calendar, meetings, projects, travel, expenses
- Family Hub: meals, chores, school, activities, shopping, health
- Research Co-pilot: literature, experiments, writing, grants, collaboration
- Founders Ops: product, engineering, hiring, investors, ops, admin, legal
steps: Add a channels field to the TemplateChoice type (string array)
Add channels to each of the 6 built-in templates (code and/or YAML depending on BUG-002 resolution)
Update tasks tool generator to accept channels from the template instead of hardcoded defaults
Update TOOLS.md generator to document the template-specific channels
Update the tasks tool help text to show the actual channels
Add test: verify each template produces a tasks tool with its own channel set
acceptanceCriteria: Each of the 6 templates defines its own channel set
Generated tasks tool validates against template-specific channels, not the hardcoded 12
TOOLS.md lists the correct channels for the selected template
Default channels (if no template) remain sensible (e.g., general, work, personal)
items.groomingNotes: UPGRADED TO MUST: The hardcoded channels (developer, trader, pa, kahu, clawdius-growth...) are from the Clawdius deployment and are actively wrong for every other template. A user who picks 'Replace my PA' sees channels named 'trader' and 'kahu'. This is a template correctness issue that undermines trust in the init output. Must fix before Phase 2 is considered complete.

items.title: Production parity — himalaya config, media mount, ollama bridge
items.description:
Three patterns exist in the production Clawdius deployment that the generator does not produce, causing agents with email/media/local-model integrations to fail silently:

1. himalaya.toml — the email tool references /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/himalaya.toml but no config template is generated. Agents with email integration get a tool that can not connect.
2. Media bind-mount — Telegram attachments need ~/.openclaw/media mounted at workspace/media. Without it, the agent can not access files users send via messaging.
3. Ollama bridge network — agents using local models via Ollama need an external docker network (ollama-bridge) in their compose. Without it, the container can not reach the Ollama API.

Each of these should be conditionally included based on integration selections.
steps: Email: generate a himalaya.toml template with CHANGE_ME placeholders for IMAP/SMTP credentials when email integration is selected
Email: add himalaya-config volume mount to generated docker-compose.yml when email integration is enabled
Media: add ~/.openclaw/media:workspace/media volume mount when messaging integration is enabled
Media: set fs.workspaceOnly to false when media mount is needed, with scoped allowed roots
Ollama: when model routing includes local/ollama, add ollama-bridge external network to generated compose
Ollama: add ollama-bridge to the networks list in generated docker-compose.yml
Add conditional logic to docker-compose generator in init/generate.ts for each pattern
Test: verify compose output includes correct mounts/networks per integration combination
acceptanceCriteria: Email integration produces himalaya.toml template + volume mount in compose
Messaging integration produces media bind-mount in compose
Local model routing produces ollama-bridge network in compose
fs.workspaceOnly correctly set based on whether media mount is needed
None of these are included when the corresponding integration is not selected
items.groomingNotes: UPGRADED TO MUST: These are silent deployment failures. Email agents produce a tool that can't connect. Media messages from Telegram are inaccessible. Local model agents can't reach Ollama. Every user who deploys with email/media/local-model integrations gets a broken agent with no error message. This is a deployment correctness bug disguised as a feature — must ship before the init flow is considered complete.

items.title: Egress audit — clawhq audit --egress from firewall logs
items.description: FEAT-012 is wontdo because ClawHQ doesn't intercept model calls. But users still need egress transparency. A firewall-based audit reads the CLAWHQ_FWD iptables LOG entries to show what domains/IPs were contacted and when — without token counts or data categories, but with enough to prove the firewall is enforcing allowlists. This covers the core privacy proof story: 'zero unexpected egress' attestation based on what the firewall actually saw. Commands: clawhq audit --egress (show domain/IP contacts from iptables LOG), --zero (attest no unexpected egress in period), --export (signed report). This is the realistic implementation of the transparency story given the architecture constraint.
items.groomingNotes: Egress audit from firewall logs — realistic privacy transparency story given wontdo on FEAT-011/012. Steps: (1) Parse iptables LOG entries from CLAWHQ_FWD chain kernel log (journalctl or /var/log/kern.log) by regex on prefix 'CLAWHQ_FWD:'; (2) Extract destination IP, port, timestamp, container ID from log fields; (3) Resolve IPs to domain names via reverse DNS; (4) Implement 'clawhq audit --egress': show domain/IP contacts sorted by time, grouped by domain; (5) Implement --zero flag: verify no unexpected egress (non-allowlisted IPs) in given period, exit 0 if clean; (6) Implement --export flag: write signed JSON report with attestation timestamp; (7) Add to status dashboard as 'network egress' section. Acceptance criteria: 'clawhq audit --egress' shows domains contacted from firewall logs; '--zero' exits 0 when only allowlisted domains contacted and 1 when unexpected egress found; '--export' produces a signed report; works gracefully when firewall logs are unavailable (warn, do not error). Note: this is network-level audit only — no token counts or data categories (that requires intercepting model calls which is out of scope). Set readyForSprint after Tech Lead adds steps/AC to the JSON record.

_dependsOn_docs: Items may include an optional 'dependsOn' array of item ID strings (e.g. "dependsOn": ["FEAT-001"]). Auto-pick skips items whose dependencies are not yet done.
